content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
March 4,
1994
Hugh Riley
City of Hollister
375 Fifth Street
Hollister,
CA
95023
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-94-023
Dear Mr.
Riley:
You have requested advice
on behalf of Hollister City
Councilmember Seth Irish regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”)
QUESTION
May Councilmember Irish participate in the decision to amend
the redevelopment plan for the City of Hollister?
CONCLUSION
Since Councilmember Irish owns
a home in the redevelopment
area,
he may not participate in the decision to amend the
redevelopment plan unless the public generally exception applies
to his real property interest and there will not be
a material
financial effect on
of his other economic interests.
FACTS
The City of Hollister is in the process of amending its
redevelopment plan.
This
amendment would increase the life of the
redevelopment agency,
add certain areas of the city to the
redevelopment area and increase the ability of the redevelopment
agency to issue debt.
Mr.
Irish is
a city councilmember in Hollister.
The City
Council of Hollister also sits as the board of directors of the
Hollister Redevelopment Agency.
Councilmember Irish’s primary
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2, Divi
sion
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—94—023
Page
2
residence is located within the original redevelopment area,
as is
a substantial portion of the city.
Both the councilinember and his wife operate businesses out of
their residence.
He has
a land surveying business
and she has
a
bookkeeping/tax business.
Both businesses are worth more than
$1,000.
Neither business pays rent for use of the residence,
which is currently listed for sale.
You have stated that none of the improvements contemplated by
the redevelopment agency is expected to create
a greater impact
on,
nor a greater benefit to,
the property or businesses of the
councilmember than would apply with respect to any other person in
the redevelopment area.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence
a governmental decision in which
the official has
a financial interest.
Section
87103 specifies that
an official has
a financial
interest within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have
a material financial ef
fect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on
the official or
a member of his or her immediate family or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has
a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has
a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of
income,
other than gifts
and other than loans by
a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business
on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty dcl
lars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received
by or promised to the public official within
12
months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director,
officer, partner,
trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
*****
Section 87103(a)—(d)


File No.
A—94—023
Page
3
According to the facts provided,
Councilmember Irish owns his
primary residence in the redevelopment area and operates his land
surveying business from his home.
The clients of his business are
a source of income to him.
In addition,
his wife also operates
her bookkeeping/tax business from their home.
(Sections
87103(a)-(d).)
Councilmember Irish is therefore prohibited from
making, participating in making or using his official position to
influence a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
2
that the decision will have a material financial effect on any of
his economic interests,
which is distinguishable from the
decision’s effect on the public generally.
Materiality
Councilmember Irish must disqualify himself from participat—
ing in any decision regarding the amendment to the redevelopment
plan if there will be a foreseeable and material financial effect
on any of his economic interests.
The Commission has adopted a
series of regulations which provide guidance concerning whether
the foreseeable financial effect of
a decision is material.
The effect of a decision on an official’s real property
interest is material if:
The decision is to designate the survey area,
to select the project area,
to adopt the
preliminary plan,
to form a project area committee,
to certify the environmental document,
to adopt the
redevelopment plan,
to add territory to the
redevelopment area,
or to rescind or amend any of
the above decisions;
and real property in which the
official has an interest,
or any part of it is
located within the boundaries
(or the proposed
boundaries)
of the redevelopment area.
Regulation 18702.1(a) (3) (0)
(Emphasis added.)
You stated that the amendment of the redevelopment plan would
add certain areas of the city to the redevelopment area.
Thus,
since the decision before the agency is to add territory to the
redevelopment area
(in which Councilmember Irish owns his home)
the financial effect of the decision is deemed material and
Councilmember Irish must disqualify himself from the decision,
unless the public generally exception applies.
2
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case.
An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is
a substantial likelihood that
it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However,
if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
(In
re Thorner
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
198.)


File No.
A—94—023
Page
4
Councilmember Irish has other economic interests which also
may be affected by the decision.
In order to determine if the
decision to amend the redevelopment plan will have
a material
financial effect on either his business or his wife’s business,
apply Regulation 18702.2.
You must also determine if there will
be a material fina.ncial effect on any sources of income, i.e.
clients,
under Regulations 18702.2 or 18702.6.
Additionally,
when the councilmember’s home is sold,
the
buyer is a source of income to Councilmember Irish for twelve
months and Councilmember Irish may not participate in any decision
which will have
a material financial effect on a source of income
to him,
pursuant to Regulation 18702.6.
The “Public Generally” Exception
A public official with an economic interest that will be
materially affected by a decision may participate in the decision
if the financial effect on the official’s economic interest is
indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
For
the “public generally” exception to apply,
a decision must affect
the official’s economic interests in substantially the same manner
as it would affect a significant segment of the public generally.
Pursuant to Regulation 18703(a):
the material financial effect of a governmental
decision on
a public official’s economic interests is
indistinguishable from its effect on the public
generally if both subdivisions
(a) (1)
and
(a) (2)
of this
regulation apply:
(1)
significant segment:
The governmental
decision will affect a “significant segment”
of the public generally as set forth below:
(A)
The decision will affect:
(i)
Ten percent or more of the
population in the jurisdiction of
the official’s agency or the
district the official represents,
or
(ii)
Ten percent or more of
all property owners, all home
owners,
or all households in the
jurisdiction of the official’s
agency or the district the official
represents,
or
(iii)
Fifty percent of all
businesses in the jurisdiction or
the district the official
represents,
so long as the segment


File No.
A—94—023
Page
5
is composed of persons other than
a
single industry, trade,
or
profession;
or,
(B)
The decision will affect 5,000
individuals who are residents of the
jur.isdiction; or,
*****
(2)
Substantially the Same Manner:
The
governmental decision will affect the official’s
economic interest in substantially the same manner
as it will affect the economic interests of the
segment identified in subdivision
(a) (1)
of this
regulation.
We do not know if the decision to amend the redevelopment
plan will affect ten percent or more of the population or property
owners, fifty percent of all the businesses or 5,000 residents in
substantially the same manner as it will affect any of the
economic interests of Councilmember Irish.
Please consult
Regulation 18703
(copy enclosed)
for guidance in making this
determination.
I trust this answers your question.
Sincerely,
Steven
G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Jij1 Stecher
Co9lsel
Legal Division
SGC:JS: ak
Enclosure

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Councilmember Irish participate in the decision to amend
the redevelopment plan for the City of Hollister?

CONCLUSION: Since Councilmember Irish owns
a home in the redevelopment
area,
he may not participate in the decision to amend the
redevelopment plan unless the public generally exception applies
to his real property interest and there will not be
a material
financial effect on
of his other economic interests.
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission March 4, 1994 Hugh Riley City of Hollister 375 Fifth Street Hollister, CA 95023 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-94-023 Dear Mr. Riley: You have requested advice on behalf of Hollister City Councilmember Seth Irish regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”) QUESTION May Councilmember Irish participate in the decision to amend the redevelopment plan for the City of Hollister? CONCLUSION Since Councilmember Irish owns a home in the redevelopment area, he may not participate in the decision to amend the redevelopment plan unless the public generally exception applies to his real property interest and there will not be a material financial effect on of his other economic interests. FACTS The City of Hollister is in the process of amending its redevelopment plan. This amendment would increase the life of the redevelopment agency, add certain areas of the city to the redevelopment area and increase the ability of the redevelopment agency to issue debt. Mr. Irish is a city councilmember in Hollister. The City Council of Hollister also sits as the board of directors of the Hollister Redevelopment Agency. Councilmember Irish’s primary 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000—18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Divi sion 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 File No. A—94—023 Page 2 residence is located within the original redevelopment area, as is a substantial portion of the city. Both the councilinember and his wife operate businesses out of their residence. He has a land surveying business and she has a bookkeeping/tax business. Both businesses are worth more than $1,000. Neither business pays rent for use of the residence, which is currently listed for sale. You have stated that none of the improvements contemplated by the redevelopment agency is expected to create a greater impact on, nor a greater benefit to, the property or businesses of the councilmember than would apply with respect to any other person in the redevelopment area. ANALYSIS Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial interest within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial ef fect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate family or on: (a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more. (b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
